behaviors.description: When a required key is absent from both the file and environment, the error message names the missing key by its exact config path
behaviors.justification: This tests error message formatting within the config loader's schema validation logic. The failure (wrong error message format) is localized to error handling and doesn't require module coordination. A unit test mocking the file operations can verify the exact format (e.g., 'database.host' not 'database_host').
behaviors.test_description: Provide a config (via mock) that is missing a required key like 'database.host'. Call the loader. Assert that the raised ConfigError message contains the exact hierarchical path 'database.host' as the missing key identifier.

behaviors.description: When XDG_CONFIG_HOME is set, the loader resolves the config file path relative to that directory instead of ~/.config
behaviors.justification: Path resolution involves environment variables, path construction, and filesystem interaction. A unit test with pure mocks could miss subtle path-building failures (e.g., incorrect separator handling, path expansion bugs on specific systems). An integration test using a real temporary directory structure will catch both the logic (respecting the env var) and correct path construction/resolution.
behaviors.test_description: Create a temporary directory with a config file inside (e.g., /tmp/myconfig/tool/config.yaml). Set XDG_CONFIG_HOME to /tmp/myconfig. Call the loader. Verify that it successfully reads the config from that location, not from ~/.config. Assert the loaded config contains expected values from the temp location file.

behaviors.description: When a TOOL_* environment variable conflicts with a config file key, the environment variable value is used
behaviors.justification: This tests precedence logic within the config loader—checking which value source (file vs. env var) has priority. The logic is self-contained and doesn't require module coordination or filesystem I/O beyond mocking. A unit test mocking the file read and setting environment variables will catch if the code gives file values precedence over env vars.
behaviors.test_description: Create a mocked config file with 'key: file_value'. Set TOOL_KEY='env_value'. Call the loader. Assert the resulting config has config['key'] == 'env_value', not 'file_value'. Verify precedence by testing multiple keys with and without env var overrides.

behaviors.description: When the config file contains a YAML syntax error, the error message includes the line number where the problem was detected
behaviors.justification: The YAML parser library provides line number information in its exceptions. The failure (missing line number in error message) is purely about whether the loader includes the parser's line number in the error message it raises. No file I/O coordination is needed; a unit test with a malformed YAML string suffices.
behaviors.test_description: Provide malformed YAML content with a syntax error on a specific line (e.g., invalid indentation on line 3). Call the loader with this content (via mock or direct YAML parsing). Assert that the raised error message contains the line number (e.g., 'line 3' or similar). Test multiple error types (invalid indentation, unclosed quotes) to verify line number reporting works across error types.

behaviors.description: When the config directory does not exist at startup, the loader creates it and writes a defaults file before proceeding
behaviors.justification: This behavior involves two operations (mkdir, write) that must occur in the correct order. A unit test with mocks might pass even if the file write is attempted before directory creation—the mock allows any operation order. An integration test using a real temporary directory will fail if operations are out of order (write to nonexistent directory fails on real OS). Integration testing also verifies that the defaults file content is correct and location is right.
behaviors.test_description: Create a temporary directory path that doesn't contain the config subdirectory yet. Set config directory env vars to point to this nonexistent path. Call the loader on first run. Assert that the config directory was created. Assert that a defaults file (config.yaml) exists at the expected path. Verify the defaults file contains valid YAML and expected default keys. Confirm that a second run does not fail even though the directory now exists.
